title: Gateway max allowances persist after router deauthorization
claim: `routerCall` grants `_gateway` a `type(uint256).max` allowance on the route token through `SmartApprove`, and that approval is never revoked after execution or when the gateway/router is removed from `availableRouters`. A previously allowlisted gateway contract therefore keeps spender rights over any approved token balance that remains on the proxy.
impact: If the proxy later holds that token again, for example from accrued protocol fees or stranded user balances, a compromised or malicious current/former gateway can still pull those funds with `transferFrom` even after operators believe it has been deauthorized.
paths: A user executes `routerCall` through an allowlisted gateway.
`SmartApprove` raises the proxy's allowance for the gateway to `type(uint256).max`.
The transaction completes, but no allowance cleanup occurs.
Later, even after `removeAvailableRouter(_gateway)`, the gateway uses its stale allowance to pull proxy-held balances of that token.

title: ERC20 routes spend the declared amount instead of the amount actually received
claim: `routerCall` transfers `_params.srcInputAmount` from the caller but never measures how many tokens the proxy actually received. Fees, approval size, and the post-call spend check are all based on the nominal input amount, so fee-on-transfer or other balance-deflating tokens can cause the proxy to spend more than the user contributed.
impact: Any pre-existing balance of the same token on the proxy, including accrued protocol fees or previously stranded funds, can be consumed to cover the shortfall. This lets an attacker use a deflationary token deposit to drain proxy reserves of that token.
paths: The proxy already holds some balance of a token that transfers less than the requested `srcInputAmount`.
An attacker calls `routerCall` with that token and a nominal amount larger than the net amount the proxy will receive.
`accrueTokenFees` and `SmartApprove` still treat the nominal amount as available and allow the downstream spender to use `_amountIn`.
The missing portion is silently funded from the proxy's existing token balance, and the balance-delta check still passes because it only verifies total tokens spent from the proxy.

title: Users can impersonate any integrator to obtain its custom fee schedule
claim: The contract trusts the user-supplied `_params.integrator` address when applying both token-fee and fixed-fee discounts. There is no authentication that the caller is actually routing through that integrator, so any user can claim the fee profile of a partner that was configured with cheaper pricing.
impact: Custom partner pricing becomes a public fee-bypass mechanism. If any integrator is configured with reduced or zero fees, arbitrary users can route under that profile and reduce or eliminate protocol revenue at scale.
paths: A manager sets discounted fee parameters for a partner with `setIntegratorInfo`.
A user submits a route while setting `_params.integrator` to that partner address.
`routerCall`/`routerCallNative` load that fee profile directly from `integratorToFeeInfo` and apply it without any proof of authorization.
The user receives the cheaper pricing while the protocol and/or intended partner segmentation loses revenue.

title: Configured per-token min/max limits are never enforced
claim: The protocol stores and exposes administrator-managed `minTokenAmount` and `maxTokenAmount` settings, but neither `routerCall` nor `routerCallNative` ever read or enforce those limits during execution.
impact: Users can bypass the configured size controls entirely and submit dust-sized or oversized routes. If operators rely on these knobs to prevent uneconomical processing or cap exposure per token, those protections are ineffective and can be griefed permissionlessly.
paths: A user submits a route whose size is below the minimum or above the maximum.
The execution paths never consult the configured bounds.
The route proceeds despite violating the configured limits.
